coredata.dc:title: Scalable flexographic printing of graphite/carbon dot nanobiosensors for non-faradaic electrochemical quantification of IL-8
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Assaifan, Abdulaziz K.
Alfadul, Hend
Albuaimi, Munira S.
Alrebaish, Abdulelah S.
Al-Gawati, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: Interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a key biomarker linked to inflammation and disability in neonates. However, current IL-8 detection methods are often costly, labor-intensive, and require highly trained personnel. While electrochemical techniques have been employed for sensitive IL-8 quantification, they typically rely on redox probes and three-electrode electrochemical cells, leading to issues such as toxicity, prolonged fabrication time, and increased waste generation. Additionally, conventional electrochemical biosensors fabrication techniques are expensive and time-consuming, limiting their scalability for mass disease screening. In this study, we introduce a low-cost, non-faradaic electrochemical nanobiosensor for the direct detection of IL-8. The sensor consists of interdigitated graphite/carbon dot conjugates flexographically printed onto a flexible polyimide substrate. The printed layer's physical properties were systematically characterized using SEM, AFM and surface profilometer, and biofunctionalization was achieved using aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and glutaraldehyde. Successful surface modification was confirmed through ATR-FTIR and EDS elemental mapping. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis demonstrated the nanobiosensor's response to varying IL-8 concentrations, with capacitance, Zmod, Zreal, and Zimag measurements. Among these, Zimag exhibited the highest sensitivity, with a response of 2.7 kΩ/log(ng/mL) and a detection limit of 50 pg/mL—well below the clinically established threshold of 600 pg/mL. This study demonstrates that, in addition to capacitance, there are multiple parameters that warrant exploration in non-faradaic biosensors to improve their sensing performance. The nanobiosensor fabrication via flexographic printing enables scalable, cost-effective production while maintaining high sensitivity and selectivity through a non-faradaic detection mechanism. This work paves the way for the development of affordable, mass-producible biosensors for early biomarker detection, facilitating timely medical intervention and improved neonatal healthcare outcomes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flexography printing
Graphite
Carbon dots
Interleukin 8
Non-faradaic biosensor
Disability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the King Salman center For Disability Research for funding this work through Research Group no KSRG-2024-102.The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Abdulaziz K. Assaifan reports financial support was provided by King Salman Center for Disability Research. Abdulaziz K. Assaifan reports a relationship with King Salman Center for Disability Research that includes: funding grants. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of flexographic printer, photograph of the printed interdigitated electrodes and a schematic of biofunctionalized biosensor.
(a,b) SEM images of graphite/carbon dots on PI substrate at low magnification and (c,d) at high magnification.

(a) AFM images of printed graphite/carbon dots, with increasing scan size from right to left (2 × 2 μm2, 5 × 5 μm2 and 10 × 10 μm2). (b) 3D AFM images of printed graphite/carbon dots, with increasing scan size from right to left (2 × 2 μm2, 5 × 5 μm2 and 10 × 10 μm2).
Surface profile of interdigitated graphite/carbon dots on top of PI substrate.
EDS elemental mapping of APTES-functionalized graphite/carbon dots on PI for (a) C, (b) O, (c) N and (d) Si.
ATR-FTIR spectrum of (a) APTES-functionalized graphite/carbon dots on PI substrate. (b) ATR-FTIR of immobilized IL-8-antibodies at the biosensor surface.

3D AFM images of (a) bare graphite/carbon dots electrode, (b) APTES-functionalized electrode, (c) glutaraldehyde-functionalized electrode and (d) IL-8-antibodies functionalized at the electrode surface.
Bode plots illustrating (a) capacitance, (b) Zmod, (c) Zreal and (d) Zimag for graphite/carbon dots-based biosensors tested against varying concentrations of IL-8.
Randle circuit for printed graphite/carbon dots nanobiosensor.
Calibration curves of (a) capacitance, (b) Zmod, (c) Zreal and (d) Zimag for graphite/carbon dots-based biosensors tested against varying concentrations of IL-8.
(a) Bare graphite/carbon dots tested against IL-8-antigens. (b) Storage stability of the biosensors. (c) Selectivity performance of the biosensor.

Bode plots illustrating Zimag response of the biosensors upon exposure to (a) low, (b) medium, and (c) high concentrations of IL-8 antigens. Corresponding changes in Zimag following incubation with (d) low, (e) medium, and (f) high concentrations of IL-8 antigens.
Fitted impedance parameters for graphite/carbon dots nanobiosensor.
List of electrochemical biosensors for IL-8 detection.

row.entry: RSD of 8.3 % was found for IL-8 using 5 biosensors.
RSD of 2.7 % was found for IL-8 using 5 immunoelectrodes.

head.ce:title: Scalable flexographic printing of graphite/carbon dot nanobiosensors for non-faradaic electrochemical quantification of IL-8

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Biomedical Technology, College of Applied Medical Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 10219, Riyadh, 11433, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a key biomarker linked to inflammation and disability in neonates. However, current IL-8 detection methods are often costly, labor-intensive, and require highly trained personnel. While electrochemical techniques have been employed for sensitive IL-8 quantification, they typically rely on redox probes and three-electrode electrochemical cells, leading to issues such as toxicity, prolonged fabrication time, and increased waste generation. Additionally, conventional electrochemical biosensors fabrication techniques are expensive and time-consuming, limiting their scalability for mass disease screening. In this study, we introduce a low-cost, non-faradaic electrochemical nanobiosensor for the direct detection of IL-8. The sensor consists of interdigitated graphite/carbon dot conjugates flexographically printed onto a flexible polyimide substrate. The printed layer's physical properties were systematically characterized using SEM, AFM and surface profilometer, and biofunctionalization was achieved using aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and glutaraldehyde. Successful surface modification was confirmed through ATR-FTIR and EDS elemental mapping. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis demonstrated the nanobiosensor's response to varying IL-8 concentrations, with capacitance, Zmod, Zreal, and Zimag measurements. Among these, Zimag exhibited the highest sensitivity, with a response of 2.7 kΩ/log(ng/mL) and a detection limit of 50 pg/mL—well below the clinically established threshold of 600 pg/mL. This study demonstrates that, in addition to capacitance, there are multiple parameters that warrant exploration in non-faradaic biosensors to improve their sensing performance. The nanobiosensor fabrication via flexographic printing enables scalable, cost-effective production while maintaining high sensitivity and selectivity through a non-faradaic detection mechanism. This work paves the way for the development of affordable, mass-producible biosensors for early biomarker detection, facilitating timely medical intervention and improved neonatal healthcare outcomes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Newborn disabilities are any abnormalities that result in significant health and growth-related problems. These disabilities are correlated with increased mortality and long-lasting complications [1]. Children born with disabilities are hospitalized more frequently compared to their peers born without disabilities, and often experience neurological and intellectual complications [2]. Many studies have shown that interleukin-8 (IL-8) is significantly associated with numerous newborn disabilities including neonatal encephalopathy [3], neonatal sepsis [4], perinatal asphyxia [5] and autism spectrum disorder (ASD) [6]. Furthermore. It was shown that elevated levels of IL-8 in human samples may indicate the presence of human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) [7]. HCMV is a major cause of disabilities in newborns and early detection would allow early medical intervention and control over newborns health as they grow up. Newborns affected by congenital infections such as cytomegalovirus often exhibit elevated levels of interleukin-8 (IL-8). This increase is thought to be part of the infant's immune response to infection acquired before birth and may be associated with a higher risk of developmental complications. Due to its connection with neurodevelopmental outcomes, IL-8 is being explored as a potential early marker for identifying newborns at risk of disability resulting from congenital infections. Elevated IL-8 levels may help flag cases where long-term impairments, such as cognitive or motor delays, are more likely to occur [8].

IL-8 is an inflammatory inducing chemokine, secreted predominantly by immune cells [9]. Its main role is the recruitment of neutrophils to the inflamed site. IL-8 also; regulates monocyte and macrophage progression and differentiation, increases oxidative metabolism and generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) possibly triggering oxidative stress and maintains homeostasis between the normal biological reactions and the pathological ones in different human systems e.g. central nervous system (CNS) [10]. Dysregulation of IL-8 levels play a significant role in neuroinflammation [11]. Chronic inflammation in response to trauma in the mature brain is different from the developing brain, the latter aggravates brain damage for years following the initial trauma possibly triggering different disabilities [12].

Currently, there are a variety of immunoassay techniques used to analyze the circulatory levels of IL-8; IMMULITE 1000 Immunoassay System [13,14], BioPlex Multiplex Immunoassays [15], electrochemiluminescence immunoassay plates [16] and sandwich ELISA based kits [17]. However, such techniques are time consuming which defeats the purpose of readily available rapid techniques [18]. To address these challenges, emerging research is ongoing to replace the above-mentioned techniques with novel, rapid and easy to use ones. Up to the present time, point of care (POC) diagnostics are mainly focused on the analysis of biomarkers utilizing a lateral-flow and colorimetric based assays. Theses assays offer only qualitative and sometimes semi-quantitative data. Lately, POC diagnostics research has shifted to quick, quantifiable biomarker detecting biosensors [19].

Biosensors have attracted lots of attention in recent years. These advanced techniques have revolutionized disease diagnosis and related biomarkers analysis. They are relatively cheaper and immensely efficient [20]. Biosensors are highly sensitive devices that detect the presence of one or more target analytes [21]. In the past, screen printed electrochemical sensors were used for the detection of IL-8, showing detection limits below 600 pg/mL [22–24]. Despite the enhanced detection limits, screen printing is still not suitable for mass production which is essential for mass screening of disease. Another study reported non-faradaic detection of IL-8 using thermally evaporated gold electrodes. Thermal evaporation is expensive and not suitable for mass production of devices either [25]. More studies reported the use of gold-based electrodes in their detection, which makes them expensive for detection of IL-8 [26,27]. Electrochemical methods such as linear sweep stripping voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry and faradaic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy are commonly used for diseases detection. While they are highly selective and offer instant results, a reference electrode is needed during analysis, demanding a larger in size sensor [28]. In non-faradaic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) sensors, reference electrode is not needed in analysis, allowing for miniaturized sensors [28]. In addition, redox probe is not necessary during analysis while operating at small alternating current voltage [29].

To overcome challenges with scalability of biosensors at low-cost, roll-to-roll printing methods such as flexography printing is an excellent choice. The flexography printing process is direct and easy-to-use, it relies on roll-to-roll, fast pace and relief printing machineries. Furthermore, it can accommodate wide range of substrates and inks. Therefore, it allows mass fabrication of biosensors at low-costs compared to other fabrication processes [30]. The combination of such printer with carbon nanomaterials such as graphite and carbon dots would significantly reduce costs associated with mass production of devices, allowing large population of patients to have access to biosensing technologies. Because of their captivating biochemical, electrical, and chemical characteristics, graphite and carbon dots are a hot topic in biosensing [31]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, characterized by their high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, chemical stability, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and facile synthesis routes, have been extensively utilized to improve biosensor performances. Their incorporation into electrochemical immunosensors enhances electron transfer kinetics, thereby improving sensitivity, accuracy, precision, and lowering the limit of detection.

[32,33]. The progress in the development of biosensors from graphite and carbon dots is very encouraging with many advantages. In the past different carbonaceous materials such as fullerenes, CNTs and graphite carbon nitride, were utilized for the detection of IL-8 using electrochemical sensors. These materials were fabricated on top of substrates using screen printing, drop casting, thermal pyrolysis of urea and covalent attachment. For mass production and subsequently mass-screening of IL-8 the fabrication methods used are not commercially viable [34].

In this work, we describe the fabrication of low-cost non-faradaic electrochemical biosensor using flexography-printed graphite and carbon dots as electrodes to detect IL-8. The physical properties of the printed graphite/carbon dots were characterized. Furthermore, IL-8-antibodies were directly immobilized on the graphite/carbon dots electrodes through (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane APTES and glutaraldehyde. APTES covalent linkage with the electrodes was confirmed with ATR-FTIR and EDS mapping. Finally, non-faradaic impedimetric measurements were carried out against IL-8-antigens in PBS solution. The biosensor demonstrated changes in different parameters including capacitance, impedance magnitude (Zmod), real impedance (Zreal), and imaginary impedance (Zimag). The measured parameters were analysed and discussed. Selectivity tests were also carried out in this study.

Conductive graphite/carbon dots ink was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Polyimide (PI) substrates with a thickness of 0.15 mm were procured from Lohmann Technologies UK. (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), glutaraldehyde (GA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Acetone, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and ethanol were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) was supplied by. Fisher Scientific, USA. IL-8-antibodies and IL-8-antigens were purchased from Abcam, UK. For selectivity tests, BSA, UL83-antigen (Milenyl Biotec Ltd., UK) and LDL-antigen (Sino Biological, China) were used.

Before the printing process, the polyimide (PI) substrates were thoroughly rinsed with acetone and IPA to ensure surface cleanliness. Flexography printing was subsequently conducted using a Flexiproof 100/UV printer manufactured by RK Printcoat Instruments, UK. Initially, the graphite/carbon dot ink was applied to a rotating anilox roller with a line count of 80 lcm−1 and a volume of 13 cm3m-2. The doctor blade ensured uniform distribution of the ink across the anilox roller's surface. Following this, the ink was transferred from the anilox roller to a printing plate affixed to the printing plate cylinder. Finally, the ink was applied from the printing plate onto the PI substrate positioned on the impression roller (see Fig. 1 ). The printed layer is an interdigitated electrode comprised of for interdigital electrodes. The width and gap of interdigital electrodes is 1 mm. The length of the interdigital electrode is 5 mm. The printing operation was conducted at a speed of 20 mm−1, and a single print was executed to deposit the graphite/carbon dots onto the substrate surface. The printed layers were dried on the PI surface by annealing on a hotplate at 300 °C for 15 min. Fig. 1 shows schematic of the printing process conducted in this study and a photograph of the printed interdigitated electrodes. After printing, characterization with field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (JSM-7600. JEOL., Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 10 kV was carried out. Atomic force microscopy (AFM), utilizing Nanoscope from Veeco-Bruker, was employed to analyze the surface morphology and derive roughness measurements of printed graphite/carbon dots. The imaging process was conducted at a scan rate of 2 Hz, with 512 samples acquired per line. Furthermore, the thickness of the printed graphite/carbon dots layer was measured using a Veeco Dektak 150 profilometer.

To immobilize IL-8-antibodies onto the surface of printed graphite/carbon dots, the sample was immersed in an ethanolic solution containing 4 % APTES at an elevated temperature of 70 °C for a duration of 10 min. After this treatment, the sample was removed, rinsed with ethanol, and dried with nitrogen. Subsequently, the sample was placed on a hot plate at 120 °C for 20 min to facilitate the cross-linking of APTES molecules with adjacent APTES molecules. To confirm the covalent linkage between APTES and the printed layers, EDS elemental mapping was carried out. Moreover, Surface analysis following APTES functionalization was performed using a Thermo Nicolet 6700 FTIR in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode, covering a spectral range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. Following this, a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde (GA) solution in PBS was applied to the biosensing surface and incubated for 1 h. The surface was then rinsed with DI water and blow-dried using nitrogen. Next, a solution of IL-8-antibodies at a concentration of 10 μg/mL in DI water was incubated on the biosensing surface for 1 h, followed by rinsing with DI water and drying with nitrogen. Finally, to minimize non-specific binding, the surface was incubated with a 5 % BSA solution in PBS for 30 min, followed by rinsing with DI water and drying with nitrogen. Fig. 1 illustrates the biofunctionalization strategy conducted in this work.

Non-faradaic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted using a GAMRY Interface 1000™ potentiostat. Before detecting IL-8, a baseline measurement was carried out. Initially, the interdigitated electrodes region, shown in Fig. 1, was incubated with 50 μL of PBS. The open circuit potential (OCP) between the two electrodes was measured to ensure system equilibrium by setting the voltage (VDC) to zero, which helped eliminate any potential bias. The OCP was recorded over a period of 100 s, followed by EIS measurements in the frequency range of 200 mHz to 10 kHz, under an applied sinusoidal perturbation of 10 mV. After baseline measurements, the PBS droplet was rinsed with deionized water and dried using nitrogen. Various concentrations of IL-8-antigen in PBS, ranging from 1 ng/mL to 10 μg/mL, were prepared and subsequently introduced to the sensing window, followed by EIS measurements. Each incubation lasted 5 min, and rinsing with deionized water was performed after every concentration incubation. Additionally, measurements were taken against successive PBS (blank) incubations. These measurements against the blanks samples are crucial for assessing the biosensor's performance, including its sensitivity and limit of detection (LoD). The alterations in capacitance, magnitude of impedance (Zmod), real impedance (Zreal) and imaginary impedance (Zimag) were normalized to the biosensor's baseline. All experiments were repeated three times for both IL-8-antigen and blank samples. Furthermore, selectivity tests were carried out against different 1 ng/mL of UL83, LDL and BSA.

Fig. 2 presents scanning electron microscope images of graphite and carbon dots on polyamide surface. Fig. 2a and b depict a uniform coverage of graphite/carbon dots at the PI substrate. Fig. 2c and d shows high magnification SEM images of interconnected carbon dots with graphite sheets. The average diameter of carbon dots is 89.5 ± 8.2 nm.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was conducted to assess the surface roughness of the printed layers. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , AFM topography was evaluated across varying scan sizes (2 × 2 μm2, 5 × 5 μm2, and 10 × 10 μm2), corroborating the carbon dots average size observed in SEM images and confirming the presence of graphite sheets beneath the carbon dots. The average surface roughness (Ra) was found to increase with the scan size, measuring 50 nm for 2 × 2 μm2, 71 nm for 5 × 5 μm2, and 80 nm for 10 × 10 μm2. Correspondingly, the root mean square surface roughness (Rq) also exhibited an upward trend, recording values of 60.4 nm for 2 × 2 μm2, 90 nm for 5 × 5 μm2, and 99 nm for 10 × 10 μm2. The roughness measurements derived from the AFM images indicate that the surface properties are well-suited for biosensing applications, as the increased surface area offers enhanced binding sites for the immobilization of IL-8 antibodies.

Fig. 4 shows the surface profile of interdigitated graphite/carbon dots electrodes measured by the Veeco Dektak 150 profilometer. The surface profile demonstrates the four interdigital electrodes deposited by the flexography printer. It is worth noting that the width and gap of interdigital electrodes on the printing plate is 1 mm. However, the surface profile shown in Fig. 4 shows that the average width of printed Graphite/carbon dots is 1.25 mm, while the average width of the PI in between the printed layers is 0.75 mm. This can be attributed to the pressure induced by the printer which causes spread of the ink beyond the desired dimensions. The average thickness of the printed graphite/carbon dots is approximately 2.5 μm.

EDS elemental mapping was carried out after functionalizing the printed layers with APTES. Fig. 5 a shows abundance of carbon (C), which is mainly attributed to the graphite and carbon dots on top of PI substrate. Additionally, the presence of oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) corresponds to the elemental composition of APTES structure. Furthermore, silicon (Si) is detected, signifying the successful functionalization of APTES and its subsequent distribution across the surface. EDX analysis show a weight% of 98.50 and 1.50 for C and O, respectively, before APTES functionalization. After APTES functionalization, the weight% became 74.09 and 16.56 for C and O, respectively. Furthermore, N and Si elements appeared with weight% of 6.76 and 2.59 for N and Si, respectively. This shows that APTES was successfully functionalized due to the presence of Si and N. In addition, the increase in O weight% can be attributed to the ethoxy group of APTES.

Fig. 6a and b illustrate the ATR-FTIR spectra of APTES functionalized on graphite/carbon dots and antibodies immobilized on the graphite/carbon dots, respectively. The spectra exhibit all the characteristic absorption peaks of APTES and antibodies. A prominent absorption band is observed at approximately 1100 cm−1, corresponding to Si–O–C [35] and Si–O–Si stretching vibrations, indicative of the Si–O–Si network formed by APTES molecules. This peak increased after antibody immobilization, merging with the CO band associated with the COOH functional groups located in the Fc region of the antibody molecules [36]. Furthermore, the band observed at 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the secondary amine group present in the immobilized antibodies [36]. Additionally, a peak at 1495 cm−1 is attributed to C–N stretching, which likely originates from amine or amide functional groups [35,37]. The absorption bands at 1700 cm−1 and 765 cm−1 are assigned to the –NH2 functional groups of APTES [35,38], further confirming its presence. After antibody immobilization, the peak at 1700 cm−1 diminished due to the interaction of glutaraldehyde with the amine groups of APTES, and a new absorption peak appeared at 1644 cm−1 related to CN of imine [39,40]. The FTIR spectrum of APTES-functionalized graphite/carbon dots also verifies the efficiency of the functionalization reaction, as evidenced by the absorption bands around 2900 cm−1, corresponding to C–H stretching vibrations from CH2 groups. The absorption bands at 2900 cm−1 increased in intensity after glutaraldehyde and antibody immobilization due to the stretching vibration of C–H bond introduced. These spectral features collectively confirm the successful incorporation of APTES and IL-8-antibodies onto the graphite/carbon dot surface [41].

Fig. 7 presents 3D AFM images corresponding to each functionalization step performed in this study. The average surface roughness (Ra) values were measured as 60.4 nm for the bare electrode, 57.1 nm after APTES functionalization, 51.1 nm following glutaraldehyde modification, and 103 nm after IL-8 antibody immobilization. Similarly, the root mean square roughness (Rq) values were 49.6 nm, 42.3 nm, 36.6 nm, and 82.8 nm, respectively, for the same sequence of functionalization steps. As observed, the surface roughness initially decreased after APTES treatment, which is attributed to the removal of impurities facilitated by incubation in an ethanol solution at 70 °C, combined with the packing effect of APTES molecules. Following glutaraldehyde modification, a decrease in surface roughness was observed, indicating successful attachment of glutaraldehyde to the APTES layer. Lastly, immobilization of IL-8 antibodies led to a significant increase in roughness, confirming the successful presence of antibodies on the electrode surface. Furthermore, the 3D surface topography obtained through AFM imaging revealed distinct morphological differences between each functionalization stage. Each step exhibited characteristic surface features with varying height profiles. Notably, the IL-8 antibody-functionalized surface displayed the highest and most prominent features compared to the earlier stages, further supporting the successful immobilization of antibodies and the formation of a biomolecular layer on the electrode.

After the successful APTES functionalization, IL-8-antibodies were immobilized at the biosensor surface through glutaraldehyde followed by blocking with BSA. Then, non-faradaic impedimetric detection was carried out against different concentrations of IL-8-antigens in PBS. Changes in capacitance, impedance magnitude (Zmod), real impedance (Zreal) and imaginary impedance (Zimag) were observed after testing against IL-8-antigens. Therefore, Bode plots for these four parameters are represented in Fig. 8 . The insets shown in Fig. 8a–d represent the capacitance, Zmod, Zreal and Zimag measurements between 200 mHz and 400 mHz. Measurements at such low frequencies reflects changes at the biosensor/samples interface. Furthermore, significant changes in measured signals were observed at 200 mHz.

It is worth noting that as the IL-8-antigen concentration increases, the capacitance increases. This is due to the direct relationship between the interfacial capacitance and the surface area of the biosensor surface. It can also be related to the increased surface charge density or alteration dielectric properties at the biosensor surface [42,43]. As the IL-8-antigen concentration increases, the surface roughness increases and hence the surface area. Zmod, Zreal and Zimag decreased because of increasing the IL-8-antigen concentration. This shows that both resistive and capacitive alterations are taking place. It is evident by the Bode plots for Zimag that the biosensor shows more non-faradaic behavior compared to the Bode plot of Zreal. As can be seen, Zimag reach around 220 kΩ compared to 40 kΩ for Zreal at around 200 mHz. Furthermore, the change in Zimag at 200 mHz when introduced with all concentrations of IL-8-antigens is around 25 kΩ compared to 2 kΩ change in Zreal when testing against all IL-8-antigen concentrations. This also indicates that the APTES passivation at the graphite/carbon dots surface was efficient for non-faradaic biosensing.

Fig. 10 a–d shows calibration curves of the measured signals. Changes in capacitance, Zmod, Zreal and Zimag were normalized to the baseline. Capacitance was increased by 400 nF when incubated with the five different IL-8-antigen concentrations. The increase in capacitance observed at low frequency (200 mHz) following IL-8 binding can be attributed to changes in the local dielectric constant at the electrode–electrolyte interface. The formation of the antigen–antibody complex introduces a proteinaceous layer that modifies the interfacial dielectric environment. Protein adsorption can increase the effective dielectric constant near the surface due to the dipolar nature of the biomolecules and their influence on interfacial polarization, especially under low-frequency conditions. This is observed with the increase in capacitance seen since the dielectric constant is directly proportional to capacitance [47]. Furthermore, the change in Zmod reached up to 27.5 kΩ. Similarly, Zimag changed by around 25.5 kΩ. On the other hand, Zreal only changed by around 2 kΩ. This shows that the change in Zreal is not as significant as the change in Zimag. This is inevitable since the biosensor is non-faradaic based. The blue solid straight line shown in the calibration curves represent the linear fittings, where sensitivities were extracted. The red solid horizontal line intercepting the y-axis represents the average change in the signal when testing the biosensors against successive incubations of PBS (blank) samples + 3 times the average standard deviations of testing the blank samples. The interception between the blue solid straight line and the red solid horizontal line leads to the limit of detection (LoD) on the x-axis. The limit of detection fluctuated for the different measured parameters. LoD is 220 pg/mL, 170 pg/mL, 340 ng/mL and 50 pg/mL for capacitance, Zmod, Zreal and Zimag, respectively. Zimag illustrated the best LoD followed by Zmod, capacitance and finally Zreal.

The sensitivity obtained from the solid blue line is 32 nf/log(ng/mL), 2 kΩ/log(ng/mL), 150 Ω/log(ng/mL) and 2.7 kΩ/log(ng/mL) for capacitance, Zmod, Zreal and Zimag, respectively. This further shows that Zimag demonstrated the best LoD and sensitivity for the biosensor. Zimag showed better LoD compared to Zmod since changes in impedance due to testing blank samples were less (see the red solid horizontal lines in Fig. 10b and d). Zmod considers both faradaic and non-faradaic signals during detection. Whereas non-faradaic biosensing mainly focuses on capacitive measurements during testing. In non-Faradaic biosensors, Zimag is a highly sensitive measure of changes at the sensor surface. When antigens bind to antibodies on the surface, they affect the local dielectric properties by altering the molecular structure, hydration, and dipole orientation. These changes increase the capacitance, which in turn causes Zimag to decrease. Unlike capacitance, which is often calculated using ideal assumptions, Zimag more directly reflects the real, complex behaviour at the interface. This makes Zimag a more sensitive indicator of biomolecular interactions, especially in systems with non-ideal surfaces.

The results obtained here recommends looking at other parameters than capacitance when analyzing non-faradaic bisoensors’ performances, since Zimag demonstrated better performances compared to other parameters.

To confirm that the observed impedance changes are due to specific antibody-antigen interactions, a control experiment was performed using bare graphite/carbon dots interdigitated electrodes without immobilized antibodies. The electrodes were exposed to five different concentrations of IL-8-antigens (10 ng/mL, 100 ng/mL, 1 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL), corresponding to the highest concentrations used in this study. As shown in Fig. 11 a, Zimag values obtained from the bare electrodes were significantly lower than those of the functionalized biosensor. The maximum change in Zimag after adding IL-8 antigens was approximately 515 Ω, much lower than the changes observed for the functionalized electrodes. Additionally, Zimag values increased with increasing antigen concentration in the case of bare electrodes, in contrast to the functionalized biosensor, where a decrease in Zimag was observed upon antigen binding. These findings confirm that the significant impedance changes in the functionalized biosensor originate from specific antibody-antigen interactions rather than nonspecific adsorption or the intrinsic properties of the electrode materials.

The storage stability of the nanobiosensors was evaluated by functionalizing the biosensors and storing them at 4 °C in a 5 % BSA solution prepared in DI water. Five biosensors were prepared for this study (Fig. 11b). The first biosensor was tested immediately on the day of functionalization, while the second, third, fourth, and fifth biosensors were tested after 2, 4, 8, and 16 days of storage, respectively. Prior to testing, each biosensor was incubated with 1 ng/mL of IL-8 antigens for 5 min, followed by non-faradaic EIS measurements. The biosensor tested on the day of functionalization exhibited a change in Zimag of 6.65 kΩ. As the storage period increased, a slight reduction in sensor response was observed. On both the 8th and 16th days, the biosensors demonstrated a change in Zimag of 6.3 kΩ. This corresponds to a decrease of approximately 5.3 % in the sensor signal over 16 days.

Interference tests were carried out using the printed nanobiosensors. Zimag was taken into account during measurmeents since it demonstrated the best biosensing performances in this study. Two approaches were undertaken for the interference (or selectivity) tests. The first approach was to test each biomarker individually. In the first approach, the concentration for each biomarker is 1 ng/mL. As can be seen in the bar chart in Fig. 11c, the change in Zimag due to testing IL-8-antigen is at least 5 times the change in Zimag when testing against UL83, LDL and BSA.

In the other approach, two samples were prepared. The first one consists of a mixture of UL83, LDL and BSA in PBS (negative samples). The other sample consists of a mixture of IL-8, UL83, LDL and BSA in PBS (positive sample|). The change in Zimag due to testing the positive sample is at least four times the change in Zimag against the negative sample (i.e. 8.2 kΩ compared to 2.02 kΩ). This shows that the developed biosensor is selective towards samples containing IL-8 antigen.

The repeatability of the printed biosensors was assessed by recording the Zimag signals of independently modified electrodes in response to IL-8 antigen. A total of 30 printed biosensors were tested, with 10 sensors evaluated at each of three IL-8 concentrations: 1 pg/mL, 1 ng/mL, and 1 μg/mL. The relative standard deviation (RSD) obtained from the biosensors tested at 1 pg/mL, 1 ng/mL, and 1 μg/mL was 4.67 %, 5.20 %, and 6.78 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the good repeatability of the biosensors across low, medium, and high concentrations of IL-8 antigen.

Fig. 12 a–f demonstrate the reproducibility of the biosensors. Non-faradaic measurements were performed for IL-8 antigen concentrations spanning three dynamic ranges: low (1 pg/mL, 10 pg/mL, 100 pg/mL), medium (1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, 100 ng/mL) and high (1 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL). Each range was tested using ten independently printed batches of biosensors. The data provided meaningful insights into the reproducibility of the biosensor. Batch-to-batch variations in the baseline Zimag were observed. The variability observed can be further minimized in future studies through control of environmental factors such as humidity, temperature and use of a vacuum environment. Average changes in Zimag of 4.5 kΩ, 12 kΩ and 23 kΩ were observed after testing the biosensor against the low, medium and high region concentrations, respectively. Changes in Zimag were reported at 200 mHz across all biosensors. RSD of the biosensors when tested against low, medium and high dynamic ranges are 15.88 %, 13.16 % and 13.93 %, respectively.

Table 2 summarizes electrochemical biosensors developed for IL-8 detection. While these biosensors demonstrate excellent limits of detection and reproducibility, their fabrication methods can be time-consuming and challenging to scale. In contrast, the flexographic printing approach presented here enables faster production speeds and is well-suited for large-scale electrode manufacturing. However, it should be noted that the reproducibility of the flexographically printed electrodes in this study is lower than that of the other biosensors listed in Table 2. This reproducibility could be improved in future work by conducting the printing process under controlled environmental conditions, such as regulated humidity, temperature, and potentially within a vacuum environment. Additionally, it is important to highlight that in all studies listed in Table 2, the biosensors were stored at 4 °C for stability testing, and in every case, they maintained at least 75 % of their original performance after two weeks. The flexography printed sensor here shows comparable LoD compared to other electrochemical biosensors fabricated by spin coating and screen printing. Furthermore, non-faradaic biosensors require only two-electrodes during detection while no redox probes are used, hence facilitating low-cost and on-site detection of biomarkers.

In this study, we report the development of low-cost, non-faradaic biosensors designed for the direct mass screening of IL-8 antigens. A grap hite/carbon dot-based flexographic printing ink was deposited onto a polyimide substrate to fabricate the sensor. Physical properties of the printed layers were found to be suitable for biosensing, while chemical characterization confirmed the successful and efficient biofunctionalization of the sensor surface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements demonstrated that the non-faradaic biosensor exhibited enhanced sensitivity and a lower limit of detection for IL-8, particularly in Zimag measurements. Additionally, the biosensor demonstrated high selectivity, ensuring reliable detection. This work establishes a foundation for the large-scale deployment of cost-effective biosensors, which could significantly enhance disease screening accessibility while promoting economic and healthcare advancements.

Abdulaziz K. Assaifan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hend Alfadul: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Munira S. Albuaimi: Methodology, Formal analysis. Abdulelah S. Alrebaish: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Mahmoud Al-Gawati: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.
The authors extend their appreciation to the King Salman center For Disability Research for funding this work through Research Group no KSRG-2024-102.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
